Using a bit of intuition, we know all the trajectories will go to either the left or right stable spiral, with the exception of the stable manifold of the saddle point. The two trajectories leading to the saddle point form the separatrices for the basins of attraction of each spiral. 6.5.13 x ¨   +   x   +   ϵ x 3   =   0 a) By checking that the isolated fixed point ( x, ẋ ) = (0,0) is the minimum of a conserved quantity E   =   1 2 x ˙ 2   −   ∫ ( − x   − ϵ x 3 )   d x   =   1 2 x ˙ 2   +   1 2 x 2   +   ϵ 4 x 4 | E x x E x x ˙ E x ˙ x E x ˙ x ˙ |   =   | 1   +   3 ϵ x 2 x ˙   +   x   +   ϵ x 3 x ˙   +   x   +   ϵ x 3 1 | ( 0 , 0 )   =   | 1 0 0 1 |   =   1   >   0 So E xx (0,0) = 1 > 0 means the fixed point is a local minimum and therefore a nonlinear center of the system. b) The origin will still be a nonlinear center for any value of ϵ because the origin is always a local minimum of E and therefore will be surrounded by closed trajectories. However, we don’t know how far away those closed trajectories exist for any value of ϵ , or even if the value of ϵ matters at all. To find out, we can look at the level sets of E for arbitrary ϵ . E =   1 2 x ˙ 2   +   1 2 x 2   +   ϵ 4 x 4 4 E ϵ =   2 ϵ x ˙ 2   +   2 ϵ x 2   =   ϵ 2   x 4 =   2 ϵ x ˙ 2   +   ( ϵ 2 x 4   +   2 ϵ x 2   +   1 )   − 1 =   2 ϵ x ˙ 2   +   ( ϵ x 2   +   1 ) 2   − 1 4 E ϵ   +   1 =   2 ϵ x ˙ 2   +   ( ϵ x 2   +   1 ) 2 This equation looks a lot like, but not quite due to the x exponent, the equations for the conic sections (hyperbola, parabola, ellipse, circle). Even more interesting is that by varying ϵ we can get all the different conic-like sections as level sets. ϵ > 0 ⇒ ellipse-like trajectories ϵ = 0 ⇒ ( x, ẋ ) = (0,0) − 1 4 E   <   ϵ   <   0   ⇒   hyperbola-like   trajectories ϵ   =   − 1 4 E   ⇒   ( x ,   x ˙ )   =   ( ± 1 − ϵ ,   0 )   =   ( ± 2 E ,   0 ) ϵ   <   − 1 4 E   ⇒   hyperbola-like   trajectories Next we can look at the graphs to see what these conic-like sections actually look like. Here ϵ > 0 and all trajectories loop around the origin no matter how far away we are. ϵ = 0 By picking different initial conditions we specify different values of E and can transition from − 1 4 E   <   ϵ   <   0 to ϵ   =   − 1 4 E to ϵ   <   − 1 4 E . We can see that the origin is still a nonlinear center, but the trajectories are no longer closed after going past the heteroclinic trajectories. 6.5.15 a) m r ϕ ¨ =
